27: arxamenos apo Mouseos kai apo panton ton propheton diermeneuen autois en pasais tais graphais ta peri heautou — beginning from Moses and all the prophets he interpreted to them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself — the risen Christ's Emmaus hermeneutic is Luke's programmatic claim about Scripture: all of it (en pasais tais graphais = in all the scriptures) is about him. The verb diermeneuen (he interpreted, explained) is the technical term for authoritative interpretation; Jesus is his own supreme interpreter. The burning hearts (v.32) indicate that the hermeneutic is not merely cognitive but spiritually transformative: right reading of the Scriptures produces encounter with the risen Christ.
44: dei plerothenal panta ta gegrammena en to nomo Mouseos kai tois prophetais kai psalmois peri emou — it is necessary for everything written in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms about me to be fulfilled — Luke's risen-Christ statement is the most comprehensive canonical claim in the Gospels: the tripartite Hebrew canon (Torah + Nevi'im + Ketuvim = the Psalms as the Writings' representative) is entirely oriented toward and fulfilled in Jesus. Dei (it is necessary) indicates divine necessity: not merely that these things happened to correspond with Scripture, but that Scripture's fulfillment was divinely required and superintended. The resurrection is the key that unlocks what was always there.
1: tē de mia tōn sabbatōn orthrou batheōs ēlthon epi to mnēma — on the first day of the week, at deep dawn, they came to the tomb. Tē mia tōn sabbatōn (the first of the week — literally the first of the sabbaths; the Jewish week was counted from the Sabbath, so “day one of the week” = Sunday, the day after Sabbath) — the day becomes the Lord’s Day for the church. Orthrou batheōs (at deep/very early dawn — bathos = depth; the deepest point of dawn, before full light) is Luke’s precise time-marker for the earliest possible morning visit. Ta arōmata (the spices they had prepared — 23:56) — the women are completing a burial interrupted by the Sabbath.
2: heuron de ton lithon apokekulismenon apo tou mnēmeiou — they found the stone rolled away from the tomb. Apokekulismenon (having been rolled away — perfect passive participle of apokuliō, to roll away) — the completed removal is already accomplished before the women arrive; they are witnesses of the effect, not the event. The stone’s removal is not to let Jesus out (the resurrection is already accomplished) but to let witnesses in; its rolled-away position is evidence, not mechanism.
3: eiselthousai de ouch heuron to sōma tou kyriou Iēsou — but when they entered they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus. Tou kyriou Iēsou (of the Lord Jesus) — the post-resurrection title used by Luke in his narrator’s voice already at the empty tomb. The combination of the full title at the moment of the missing body is deliberately ironic: the one whose body cannot be found is identified by the highest Christological title. The absence of the body is the first resurrection evidence: the tomb is empty, and the Lord’s body is gone.
4: kai egeneto en tō aporein autas peri toutou kai idou andres dyo epestēsan autais en esthēti astraptousē — while they were puzzled/perplexed about this, suddenly two men in dazzling clothes stood beside them. Aporein (to be perplexed, at a loss — the same root as aporia of 21:25) — the women’s confusion is epistemic: the evidence does not fit their categories. Dyo andres (two men) — Luke consistently represents angelic messengers as “men” (cf. 24:23 where they are called “a vision of angels”). Esthēti astraptousē (dazzling/lightning-like clothing) — the theophanic language of heavenly beings whose garments reflect divine glory.
5: emphobōn de genomenōn autōn kai klinousōn ta prosōpa eis tēn gēn eipan pros autas: ti zēteite ton zōnta meta tōn nekrōn — in their fright, the women bowed their faces to the ground. The men said to them: Why do you seek the living among the dead? Ti zēteite ton zōnta meta tōn nekrōn (why do you seek the living one among the dead ones?) — the question is the resurrection’s first announcement, phrased as a gentle reproof of a category error: the women are looking for Jesus among the dead (nekrōn), but he is the living one (ton zōnta — the present participle: the one who is alive, the living one, a title). The question presupposes the resurrection as already accomplished.
6: ouk estin hōde, alla ēgerthē — he is not here, he has been raised! The double declaration is Luke’s resurrection kerygma in its most compressed form. Ouk estin hōde (he is not here) — the negative fact: absence from the tomb. Alla ēgerthē (but he has been raised — divine passive of egeirō, to raise; God raised him) — the positive claim: resurrection accomplished by divine action. Mnēsthēte hōs elalēsen hymin eti ōn en tē Galilaia (remember how he spoke to you while he was still in Galilee) — the command to remember redirects the women to Jesus’s own predictions, making the resurrection the fulfillment of his word rather than a surprise.
7: legōn ton huion tou anthrōpou hoti dei paradothēnai eis cheiras anthrōpōn hamartolon kai staurōthēnai kai tē tritē hēmera anastēnai — saying that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, be crucified, and on the third day be raised again. The passion-resurrection summary (paradothēnai... staurōthēnai... anastēnai — delivered, crucified, raised) repeats the triadic pattern of the passion predictions (9:22; 9:44; 18:32-33). Dei (must — divine necessity) frames the entire sequence as providentially required, not accidentally suffered. Eis cheiras anthrōpōn hamartolon (into the hands of sinful men) — the human agency of the crucifixion is named without exonerating it.
8: kai emnēsthēsan tōn rhēmatōn autou — and they remembered his words. The remembering (emnēsthēsan — aorist of mimnēskomai) is the cognitive breakthrough that makes the evidence intelligible: the empty tomb alone is ambiguous; the empty tomb plus the remembered prediction produces understanding. Luke’s resurrection epistemology consistently pairs evidence with the interpretive key of Jesus’s prior teaching: the disciples need both the fact and the framework. The women’s remembering is the model of post-resurrection faith: connect what you see to what he said.
9: kai hypostrepsasai apo tou mnēmeiou apēngeilan tauta panta tois hendeka kai pasin tois loipois — when they returned from the tomb, they told all these things to the Eleven and to all the others. The women are the first witnesses of the resurrection and the first proclaimers of the resurrection message — a culturally significant detail in a context where women’s legal testimony was systematically discounted. Tois hendeka kai pasin tois loipois (the Eleven and all the others) — the broader community of disciples beyond the Twelve (now Eleven after Judas’s defection); the resurrection announcement goes to the whole community.
10: ēsan de hē Magdalēnē Maria kai Iōanna kai Maria hē Iakōbou kai hai loipai syn autais — it was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the others with them who told this to the apostles. Luke names three of the women witnesses: Mary Magdalene (present also in Mark 16:1; Matt 28:1; John 20:1), Joanna (unique to Luke; also named in 8:3 as the wife of Chuza, Herod’s steward), and Mary the mother of James (also in Mark 16:1). The named women anchor the resurrection tradition in specific, identifiable witnesses; the claim is not anonymous but anchored in known persons.
11: kai ephainonto enōpion autōn hōsei lēros ta rhēmata tauta, kai ēpistoun autais — but they did not believe the women, because their words seemed to them like nonsense. Lēros (nonsense, idle talk — a medical term for the delirious chatter of a fever patient; Hippocratic literature uses it for the rambling speech of the seriously ill) — Luke uses the medical vocabulary of delirium to describe the apostles’ dismissal of the resurrection report. The use of this specific term is likely intentional: Luke the physician (Col 4:14) reaches for the clinical word for irrational speech. Ēpistoun autais (they disbelieved them) — the male disciples’ initial unbelief in the female witnesses is a mark of historical honesty; no one inventing a resurrection story would begin it with the primary witnesses being disbelieved.
12: ho de Petros anastas edramen epi to mnēmeiou kai parakupsas blepei ta othonia mona — Peter, however, got up and ran to the tomb. Bending over, he saw the strips of linen lying by themselves. Edramen (ran) — Peter’s response to the disbelieved report is immediate and urgent: he runs. Ta othonia mona (the linen wrappings alone, by themselves) — the burial cloths are present but the body is absent; the wrappings have not been removed and carried away with the body, which would indicate theft; they lie as if the body evaporated from within them. Apēlthen pros heauton thaumazōn to gegonos (he went away wondering at what had happened) — Peter’s response is wonder (thaumazōn), not yet belief; he processes the evidence without yet arriving at the resurrection conclusion.
13: kai idou dyo ex autōn ēsan poreuomenoi en autē tē hēmera eis kōmēn apechousan stadious hexēkonta apo Ierousalēm — that same day two of them were going to a village called Emmaus, about sixty stadia from Jerusalem. En autē tē hēmera (on that same day) — the Emmaus journey happens on the resurrection day itself; the chronology is tight. Stadious hexēkonta (sixty stadia = approximately 7 miles; a stade is about 607 feet) — Luke gives the precise distance; the walk would take about 2-3 hours each way. Emmaoūs — the village’s exact location is debated; ancient candidates include Nicopolis (modern Imwas, 18 miles) and Qubeibeh (about 7 miles), the latter fitting the 60-stade figure better.
14: kai autoi hōmiloun pros allēlous peri pantōn tōn symbebēkotōn toutōn — they were talking with each other about everything that had happened. Hōmiloun (were conversing, talking — the verb from which “homily” derives; a continuous, back-and-forth discussion) — the two disciples are engaged in sustained analysis of the crucifixion events. Tōn symbebēkotōn toutōn (these things that had happened — perfect participle of symbaínō, to come together, to occur) — the events are complete in their occurrence but not yet understood; the disciples are working through the cognitive aftermath of the cross.
15: kai egeneto en tō homilein autous kai syzētein kai autos Iēsous engisas syneporeueto autois — as they talked and discussed these things, Jesus himself came near and walked along with them. Autos Iēsous (Jesus himself — the emphatic pronoun marks the dramatic arrival: not a report about Jesus, not a vision of Jesus, but Jesus himself in person) draws near and begins walking with them. Syneporeueto autois (traveled along with them — the verb of journeying together on the same road) — the Emmaus narrative is a journey-with-Jesus story; the risen Lord meets the disciples in the midst of their ordinary travel and their confused conversation.
16: hoi de ophthalmoi autōn ekratounto tou mē epignōnai auton — but their eyes were kept from recognizing him. Ekratounto (were kept, held — imperfect passive of krateō, to hold firmly, grip) — the divine passive: God actively prevented their recognition. Tou mē epignōnai auton (from recognizing him — the genitive articular infinitive of purpose/result) — the non-recognition is not a failure of perception but a divinely imposed barrier. The Emmaus narrative builds toward the moment of recognition (v.31) as its structural climax; the prevented recognition creates the space for the Scripture-opening and the eucharistic gesture that produce genuine understanding rather than mere visual identification.
17: eipen de pros autous: tines hoi logoi houtoi hous antiballete pros allēlous peripatountes kai estē skythrōpoi — he asked them: What are you discussing as you walk along? They stood still, their faces downcast. Antiballete (you are throwing back and forth, exchanging — the verb for a dialogue where each party responds to the other) — Jesus uses the image of conversational exchange. Estēsan skythrōpoi (they stood, gloomy/sad-faced — skythrōpos is a NT rare word for a gloomy, downcast expression; used also in Matt 6:16 for the ostentatious sad-face of fasting hypocrites) — their physical posture and facial expression embody the grief and confusion of the post-crucifixion period.
18: apokritheis de heis onomati Kleopas eipen pros auton: sy monos paroikeis Ierousalēm kai ouk egnōs ta genomena en autē en tais hēmerais tautais — one of them, named Cleopas, answered: Are you the only stranger in Jerusalem who doesn’t know the things that happened there in these days? Kleopas — one of the two named disciples; his companion remains unnamed. Cleopas may be an alternate form of Clopas/Alpheus; some church fathers (Hegesippus via Eusebius) identify him as a brother or relative of Joseph. Sy monos paroikeis (you alone are staying as a stranger/sojourner) — the irony is complete: the one they think is ignorant of recent events is the event himself.
19: kai eipen autois: poia; hoi de eipan autō: ta peri Iēsou tou Nazarēnou — what things? he asked. About Jesus of Nazareth, they replied. Iēsou tou Nazarēnou (Jesus the Nazarene — the geographic identification that had become the standard way of distinguishing this Jesus from others; used in the early church as a formal identification, Acts 2:22; 3:6) — the disciples’ identification of Jesus is geographically precise and shows that “Jesus of Nazareth” was already a recognized identity label. Dynatos en ergō kai logō enantion tou theou kai pantos tou laou (powerful in deed and word before God and all the people) — the twofold power of Jesus: the deed-word combination is the prophetic pattern, and the testimony is before both God (coram Deo) and all the people (public, verifiable).
20: hopōs te paredōkan auton hoi archiereis kai hoi archontes hēmōn eis krima thanatou kai estaurōsan auton — the chief priests and our rulers handed him over to be sentenced to death, and they crucified him. The disciples’ summary places the responsibility on the Jewish leaders (hoi archiereis kai hoi archontes) who handed him over (paredōkan — the passion’s central verb of betrayal-transfer) and the Roman execution (estaurōsan, they crucified him). The political anatomy of the crucifixion is accurately summarized: Jewish authorities initiated, Roman authorities executed. The disciples state the facts without yet understanding their redemptive meaning.
21: hēmeis de elpizomen hoti autos estin ho mellōn lytrousthai ton Israēl — but we had hoped that he was the one who was going to redeem Israel. Ēlpizomen (we were hoping — imperfect indicative, the tense of continuous past action now broken off) — the imperfect marks the death of their hope: “we used to hope, but that hope is now over.” Ho mellōn lytrousthai ton Israēl (the one who was about to redeem Israel — lytrousthai is the verb of ransom-liberation from Exodus typology; Zechariah used it in the Benedictus, 1:68) — they had understood Jesus in terms of national-political redemption; the cross destroyed that particular hope. Their framework was right (redemption) but too narrow (Israel only, this-worldly liberation).
22: alla kai gynai kes tines ex hēmōn exestēsan hēmas — in addition, some of our women amazed us. Exestēsan hēmas (they amazed us, astonished us — from existēmi, to stand outside oneself; the verb for astonishment or amazement) — the women’s report has disrupted the disciples’ settled grief without resolving it. The “women’s evidence” is positioned as an intermediate stage: it creates confusion without yet producing faith. The Emmaus disciples are in the uncomfortable position between the crucifixion’s finality and the women’s unbelievable report.
23: kai mē heurousai to sōma autou ēlthon legousai kai optasian angelōn heōrakenai hoi legousin auton zēn — but they didn’t find his body. They came and told us that they had seen a vision of angels, who said he was alive. Optasian angelōn (a vision of angels — the word optasia is used for visionary experience; the Emmaus disciples report the women’s encounter as a “vision” rather than as objective events, revealing their own epistemic skepticism) — even the women’s encounter with the angelic messengers is processed through the Emmaus disciples’ doubt-framework as a possibly subjective vision. The report is accurate; the framing as “vision” reveals the hearers’ resistance.
24: kai apēlthon tines tōn syn hēmin epi to mnēmēion kai heuron houtōs kathōs kai hai gynaikes eipon, auton de ouk eidon — then some of our companions went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said, but they did not see him. The male disciples’ verification of the tomb condition is corroborated (found it just as the women said: empty) but incomplete (did not see him). Peter’s investigation (v.12) is here placed in a wider corroborating frame. The cumulative weight of evidence — empty tomb, abandoned linen, angels’ testimony, confirmed by multiple witnesses — has not yet produced resurrection faith; the disciples need the Scriptures opened and the Lord recognized.
25: kai autos eipen pros autous: ō anoētoi kai bradeis tē kardia tou pisteuein epi pasin hois elalēsan hoi prophētai — he said to them: How foolish you are, and how slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Anoētoi (foolish, without understanding — a = not + noetikos = intelligently reasoning; those who fail to exercise the capacity for rational understanding) — the rebuke is intellectual: the disciples have failed to think through the prophetic testimony. Bradeis tē kardia tou pisteuein (slow of heart to believe — the heart as the seat of understanding in Semitic thought; slowness of heart = reluctance to draw the full implications of what the evidence demands). The prophets had provided sufficient testimony for faith; the disciples’ failure is not lack of evidence but slow-heartedness.
26: ouchi tauta edei pathein ton Christon kai eiselthein eis tēn doxan autou — was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and then enter into his glory? The rhetorical question has the force of a Christological argument: ouchi edei (was it not necessary — the dei of divine necessity framing the entire passion-resurrection as the scripturally required pattern). Pathein ton Christon (for the Christ to suffer) — the necessity of a suffering Messiah is the interpretive breakthrough the Emmaus disciples had missed: their Messianic framework excluded suffering, but the prophetic testimony required it. Eiselthein eis tēn doxan autou (to enter into his glory) — suffering is not the Messiah’s defeat but the pathway to glory, the road through which the resurrection glory is reached.
28: kai ēngisan eis tēn kōmēn hou eporeuonto, kai autos prosepoieito porrōteron poreuesthai — as they approached the village to which they were going, Jesus acted as if he were going further. Prosepoieito porrōteron poreuesthai (he made as if to go further — prospoieomai with an infinitive: to pretend, to act as if) — Jesus does not force an invitation; he makes a movement that requires the disciples to choose whether to extend hospitality. The hospitality pattern (meinon meth hēmōn, stay with us) is an echo of the OT hospitality traditions and foreshadows the eucharistic recognition that will follow.
29: kai parebiasanto auton legontes: meinon meth hēmōn, hoti pros esperan estin kai keklinēken ēdē hē hēmera — but they strongly urged him: Stay with us, for it is nearly evening; the day is almost over. Parebiasanto (they urged strongly, pressed him — para- + biazō = to force alongside; to press with force; the most urgent form of invitation) — the disciples do not merely invite but press Jesus to remain. Pros esperan estin kai keklinēken hē hēmera (it is toward evening and the day has declined/bent down) — the temporal urgency of the invitation: travel after dark is dangerous. The urgency will be fulfilled in a different sense: the day’s ending becomes the moment of recognition and new beginning.
30: kai egeneto en tō kataklithēnai auton met autōn labōn ton arton eulogēsen kai klasas epedidou autois — when he was at table with them, he took bread, gave thanks, broke it and began to give it to them. The four eucharistic verbs (labōn: took; eulogēsen: gave thanks/blessed; klasas: broke; epedidou: gave) precisely mirror Luke’s Last Supper account (22:19) and the feeding of the five thousand (9:16). The guest becomes the host; the one who was invited to stay assumes the role of the meal’s presider. Luke frames the Emmaus recognition as a eucharistic event: the breaking of bread is the context of resurrection recognition.
31: autōn de diēnoichthēsan hoi ophthalmoi kai epegnōsan auton kai autos aphantos egeneto ap autōn — then their eyes were opened and they recognized him, and he disappeared from their sight. Diēnoichthēsan hoi ophthalmoi (their eyes were opened — divine passive: God opened their eyes) — the miracle of recognition is the reversal of v.16’s miracle of non-recognition; both are divinely controlled. Epegnōsan auton (they recognized him — epiginōskō is the full, complete knowing that goes beyond simple identification) — the recognition is complete. Aphantos egeneto (he became invisible, disappeared — aphantos is a NT hapax meaning unseen, invisible; from a + phaínō = to appear) — the instant disappearance marks the post-resurrection mode of existence: not bound by physical location.
32: kai eipan pros allēlous: ouchi hē kardia hēmōn kaiomenē ēn en hēmin hōs elalei hēmin en tē hodō, hōs diēnoigen hēmin tas graphas — they said to each other: Were not our hearts burning within us while he talked with us on the road and opened the Scriptures to us? Hē kardia hēmōn kaiomenē ēn (our heart was burning — imperfect, the continuing inner experience during the journey) — the burning heart is the phenomenology of encounter with the risen Christ through Scripture: before they recognized him visually, they were experiencing the unmistakable inner warmth of genuine divine encounter. Hōs diēnoigen hēmin tas graphas (as he opened the Scriptures to us — dianoigō is to open wide, to unlock what was closed) — the Scripture-opening produces the burning recognition that precedes and enables the visual recognition at the table.
33: kai anastantes autē tē hōra hypostrepsan eis Ierousalēm kai heuron ēthroismenous tous hendeka kai tous syn autois — they got up that very hour and returned to Jerusalem. There they found the Eleven and those with them, assembled together. Autē tē hōra (that very hour — the immediacy of the response: they do not wait until morning despite the evening hour) — the burning hearts and the opened minds produce immediate action; the resurrection news cannot be kept. The return journey to Jerusalem reverses the grief-departure: they had been going away from Jerusalem in confusion; they now return to it with joy.
34: legontas hoti ontōs ēgerthē ho kyrios kai ōphthē Simōni — saying: It is true! The Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon. The assembled community greets the two Emmaus disciples with their own prior confirmation: ontōs ēgerthē (truly/really he has been raised — ontōs is the adverb of ontological reality: genuinely, in fact, truly) — the resurrection is not rumor but confirmed fact. Ōphthē Simōni (he appeared to Simon — the passive of horaō, to see, used as the technical term for resurrection appearances; cf. 1 Cor 15:5 where the same verb appears) — the appearance to Peter is corroborated in 1 Cor 15:5 as the first individual appearance; Luke mentions it without narrating it, a significant example of restrained tradition.
35: kai autoi exēgounto ta en tē hodō kai hōs egnōsthē autois en tē klasei tou artou — then the two told what had happened on the road, and how Jesus was recognized by them in the breaking of the bread. En tē klasei tou artou (in the breaking of the bread) — Luke’s phrase for the eucharistic action that produced recognition. The phrase recurs in Acts 2:42,46 as a regular practice of the early Jerusalem community: the breaking of bread as the habitual context of the risen Christ’s presence with his community. The Emmaus narrative provides the theological grounding: the Lord is known in the eucharistic breaking.
36: tauta de autōn lalountōn autos estē en mesō autōn kai legei autois: eirēnē hymin — while they were still talking about this, Jesus himself stood among them and said to them: Peace be with you. Autos estē en mesō autōn (he himself stood in their midst) — the appearance is sudden and unannounced; the closed doors are not mentioned (contrast John 20:19-20) but implied by the suddenness of the appearance. Eirēnē hymin (peace to you — the standard Jewish greeting shalom lekhem) — the risen Lord’s first word to the assembled community is the covenant greeting of wholeness and wellbeing; the peace of his resurrection becomes the peace he gives his community.
37: ptoēthentes de kai emphoboi genomenoi edokoun pneuma theōrein — they were startled and frightened, thinking they saw a ghost. Ptoēthentes (startled, terrified — ptoeō is to frighten, to cause to flee in terror; Luke uses it also in 21:9) and emphoboi (full of fear, frightened) — the double fear-word intensifies the disciples’ response: not mere surprise but genuine terror. Pneuma theōrein (they were thinking they were seeing a ghost/spirit) — the disciples’ misidentification is the apologetic occasion: they think they are seeing a disembodied spirit, not a physically resurrected person. Jesus’s response (vv.38-43) is a systematic refutation of that interpretation.
38: kai eipen autois: ti tetaragmenoi este kai dia ti dialogismoi anabainousin en tē kardia hymōn — he said to them: Why are you troubled, and why do doubts rise in your minds? Tetaragmenoi (troubled, disturbed — perfect passive participle of tarasso, to stir up, agitate) — the interior disturbance of confused emotion. Dialogismoi anabainousin en tē kardia hymōn (reasonings/doubts rise up in your heart — dialogismos is internal reasoning/deliberation that produces doubt; “rising up” in the heart suggests the involuntary surge of questioning thoughts) — Jesus diagnoses the dual problem: emotional disturbance and cognitive doubt, both of which he will address through sensory evidence in vv.39-43.
39: idete tas cheiras mou kai tous podas mou hoti egō eimi autos; psēlaphēsate me kai idete — look at my hands and my feet. It is I myself! Touch me and see. Tas cheiras mou kai tous podas mou (my hands and feet) — the hands and feet bear the marks of crucifixion nails; the invitation to examine them is an invitation to identify the risen Jesus as the crucified Jesus — the resurrection is the resurrection of the same body, not a new spiritual form. Psēlaphēsate me kai idete (touch me and see — psēlaphāō is tactile exploration, the touch of one who wants to verify; the same verb used in Acts 17:27 for seeking God) — touch is the most direct refutation of ghost-interpretation.
40: kai touto eipōn edeixen autois tas cheiras kai tous podas — when he had said this, he showed them his hands and feet. The showing of hands and feet is the confirmatory act accompanying the verbal invitation of v.39: Jesus’ words (look, touch) are followed by the gesture (showed) that makes the examination possible. The text is absent from some important Western manuscripts (D, Old Latin) but present in the Alexandrian tradition; most scholars consider it original. The physical display is Luke’s apologetic for bodily resurrection: the risen Lord has identifiable physical marks.
41: eti de apistountōn autōn apo tēs charas kai thaumazontōn eipen autois: echete ti brōsimon enthade — and while they still disbelieved from joy and amazement, he asked them: Do you have anything here to eat? Apistountōn apo tēs charas kai thaumazontōn (while they were disbelieving from joy and amazement) — the paradoxical psychological state: the disciples cannot fully process what they are seeing because the joy of it is too overwhelming; disbelief and joy co-exist as the mind struggles to accept what is too good to be true. The request for food is the climax of the bodily-resurrection apologetic: a ghost cannot eat; a risen body can and does.
42: hoi de epedōkan autō ichthuos optou meros — they gave him a piece of broiled fish. Ichthuos optou meros (a piece of broiled/grilled fish — optos means cooked by fire, roasted, grilled) — the specific detail (fish, not bread; grilled, not baked) grounds the account in concrete sensory memory. Fish is an appropriate Jerusalem meal (transported from the Sea of Galilee); its preparation (optos) specifies the cooking method. The specificity of the fish and its preparation is a hallmark of eyewitness memory preservation.
43: kai labōn enōpion autōn ephagen — and he took it and ate it in their presence. Enōpion autōn (before them, in their presence — in plain sight, with full visibility) — the eating is a public, observable act. Ephagen (ate — the simple aorist: he ate it, fully, completely) — the eating of food is the most direct demonstration of bodily resurrection: a spirit-appearance cannot eat physical food; the risen Jesus eats in full view of the assembled disciples. Luke’s bodily-resurrection apologetic is complete with this verse: visible scars, tangible body, consumed food.
45: tote diēnoixen autōn ton noun tou synienai tas graphas — then he opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures. Diēnoixen autōn ton noun (he opened their mind/understanding — the same verb dianoigō used for the Scripture-opening in v.32) — the opening of the Scriptures (v.32) and the opening of the mind (v.45) are parallel acts of the risen Lord: the external text and the internal capacity for understanding are both opened by him. Without the opened mind, the opened Scriptures cannot be understood; the hermeneutical event requires both the text and the reader’s transformation.
46: hoti houtōs gegraptai pathein ton Christon kai anastēnai ek nekrōn tē tritē hēmera — this is what is written: The Messiah will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day. The summary of what is written covers the entire passion-resurrection as a single scriptural statement: pathein ton Christon (the Messiah to suffer) and anastēnai ek nekrōn tē tritē hēmera (to rise from the dead on the third day). No single OT text contains this precise formulation; Jesus is presenting the cumulative witness of the whole canonical pattern. Paul makes the same move in 1 Cor 15:3-4 (kata tas graphas twice: according to the Scriptures, both for death and resurrection).
47: kai kērychthēnai epi tō onomati autou metanoian eis aphesin hamartiōn eis panta ta ethnē — and repentance for the forgiveness of sins will be preached in his name to all nations. Metanoian eis aphesin hamartiōn (repentance for/leading to forgiveness of sins — metanoia = the change of mind and direction that is the human response to the gospel; aphesis = release, forgiveness — the term introduced in 4:18 as the Jubilee-release Jesus came to proclaim) — the Lukan summary of the apostolic preaching that will fill Acts: beginning at Jerusalem (arxamenōn apo Ierousalēm), going to all nations (eis panta ta ethnē). The scope is universal; the center is Jesus’s name (epi tō onomati autou).
48: hymeis martyres toutōn — you are witnesses of these things. Martyres (witnesses — the legal term for one who testifies to what they have seen; the source of the word “martyr” after witnesses began dying for their testimony) — the apostolic identity as witnesses is the foundation of the church’s mission in Acts. Toutōn (of these things) — the testimony is to the entire preceding account: the passion, the empty tomb, the appearances, the opened Scriptures. The witness-commission without the Spirit’s empowerment (v.49) would be insufficient; the two go together as commission and equipment.
49: kai idou egō apostellō tēn epangelian tou patros mou eph hymas — and behold, I am going to send what my Father promised upon you. Tēn epangelian tou patros mou (the promise of my Father) — the promised Spirit (Joel 2:28-32; Isa 32:15; 44:3; Ezek 36:26-27) for which John the Baptist had prepared (3:16: he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire). Luke does not name the Spirit here; the reference is sufficiently clear from the promise tradition. Hymeis de kathisate en tē polei heōs hou endysēsthe ex hypsous dynamin (but you, stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high) — endysēsthe (to be clothed with) is the clothing metaphor for being enveloped by the Spirit’s power; ex hypsous (from on high) echoes Isa 32:15 and anticipates Pentecost.
50: exēgagen de autous exō heōs pros Bēthanion kai eparas tas cheiras autou eulogēsen autous — when he had led them out to the vicinity of Bethany, he lifted up his hands and blessed them. The ascension-blessing narrative (unique to Luke among the Gospels) takes place near Bethany, on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives (Acts 1:12 gives the location as the Mount of Olives; Bethany is at the foot of its eastern slope). Eparas tas cheiras autou eulogēsen autous (lifting up his hands he blessed them) — the priestly gesture: the high priestly blessing (Num 6:22-27; Sir 50:20-22) involved raised hands. The ascension is structured as a priestly act of blessing; Jesus departs as priest blessing his people.
51: kai egeneto en tō eulogein auton autous diestē ap autōn kai anephereto eis ton ouranon — while he was blessing them, he left them and was taken up into heaven. Diestē ap autōn (he withdrew, separated himself from them — diistamai = to stand apart) and anephereto eis ton ouranon (he was carried up into heaven — anaferō in the passive: to be carried/borne upward; the same verb used for Elijah’s being “taken up” in the LXX tradition) — the ascension is an act of being taken rather than of going: passive verbs throughout. The ascension happens during the blessing, so the disciples are blessed in the moment of departure; the departure is itself a blessing-moment. Luke narrates it briefly here; Acts 1:9-11 gives the fuller account.
52: kai autoi proskynēsantes auton hypostrepsan eis Ierousalēm meta charas megalēs — then they worshipped him and returned to Jerusalem with great joy. Proskynēsantes auton (having worshipped him — proskyneō is the full prostration-worship of deity; the disciples worship the ascended Jesus as divine) — the ascension produces worship rather than grief: the departure of the risen Lord is experienced as a cause for joy and worship, not mourning. Meta charas megalēs (with great joy) — the characteristic Lukan theme of joy (also 1:14,44; 2:10; 10:17; 15:7,10; 19:6,37; 24:41) now at its eschatological peak: the resurrection and ascension complete the joy that the births of John and Jesus began.
53: kai ēsan dia pantos en tō hierō eulogountes ton theon — and they were continually in the temple, praising God. The Gospel of Luke ends where it began: in the temple (en tō hierō). Zechariah’s priestly service in the temple (1:5-23) opens the narrative; the disciples’ continual temple praise closes it. Dia pantos (continually, always — the same phrase used in 24:10 for the women) — the community of disciples is defined by ceaseless temple-praise. Eulogountes ton theon (blessing/praising God — the Benedictus verb of 1:64,68) completes the inclusio: the blessing of God with which Zechariah’s tongue was released is now the continuous activity of the entire community of resurrection witnesses.
